retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical management can take place using an intraoral or extraoral approach, the choice of which depends mainly on the site and type of body fracture. Simple and fractures in the anterior segment with no or only slight dislocation should preferably have treatment using an intraoral approach. [15] This approach provides excellent access to the fracture site and allows observing the occlusion to reduce fracture and application of rigid fixation. The incision is placed in the vestibular region approximately 5 to 7 mm below the mucogingival junction to facilitate closure. This location also aids in the prevention of wound dehiscence. During an intraoral approach, care is necessary to avoid injuring the mental nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction techniques include intraoral techniques and extraoral techniques. Intraoral techniques include the bimanual, wrist pivot, and recumbent position. Extraoral techniques protect the provider's fingers from being bitten. Extraoral techniques include the gag reflex method, the syringe method, and the external approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hospital admission may be indicated in patients with significantly displaced fractures. Patients with airway compromise, patients who are unable to tolerate oral feeding or secretions, and patients with inadequate pain control will also require hospital admission. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Facial Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Mandible Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: MMF duration is generally shorter (2 to 3 weeks) than recommended for adults to avoid TMJ ankylosis. Applying MMF in edentulous young children may involve circummandibular wiring and wiring through the piriform aperture to secure splints for stabilization. [37] Primary teeth may be used for MMF. However, wiring arch bars to loose teeth must be avoided in patients aged 6 to 12. Monocortical screws are preferred if rigid fracture fixation is required to minimize the risk of injury to unerupted secondary teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Type A3 (infraorbital rim): The infraorbital rim should be reduced and plated to restore its normal contour and approached via either a transconjunctival or a subciliary lower eyelid incision.

retrieved_docs.title: Naso-Orbito-Ethmoid Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In type 3 NOE complex fractures, reduction of the MCT (canthopexy) is achieved via transnasal wiring, which is performed by drilling a small hole into the medial orbital wall and tethering the MCT with a wire. The use of a needle to secure the MCT in lieu of a drill is sometimes necessary for unstable comminuted fractures of the medial orbital wall. [24] As a general rule, the intercanthal distance should be overcorrected, i.e., narrowed beyond the normal intercanthal distance to compensate for the lateralization that inevitably occurs postoperatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Even though popularly used, facial muscle energy is an active and direct intervention to the pathological barrier. The muscle energy technique requires the physician to employ some emphatic forces. The physician must take the responsibility to reconsider this intervention if the patient has the following: Low vitality Jaw fractures Unstable joints Acute surgical record MFR, although passive, can be a direct or indirect intervention. Therefore, MFR is unsuitable for patients of all ages and conditions. The physician must reconsider treatment if the patient has: Healing fractures Advanced diabetes Severe osteoporosis Rheumatoid arthritis Malignancy Aneurysm BLT is a passive, direct/ indirect intervention. It is much more tolerable compared to other active alternatives. However, some patients may not be suitable for the procedure if the patient: Refuses consent Has acute jaw fractures Shows temporal bone malignancies Has a history of osteomyelitis [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team approach to the management of the patient during all aspects of the patient’s care helps the patient to have expert treatment resulting in the best possible outcome. [Level IV]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with mandibular fractures frequently have associated injuries. Initial treatment should be directed toward immediate, potentially life-threatening injuries such as airway obstruction, major hemorrhage, cervical spine injury, and intracranial injury. If oral intubation cannot be performed, indicated surgical airway may be required. Nasotracheal intubation should not be performed if midface, nasal, or anterior skull base fractures are suspected. Patients with mandibular fractures should observe cervical spine precautions until cleared. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular fractures are often associated with other significant injuries. In some instances, the airway may be compromised. Care of trauma patients is best achieved with an interprofessional team of clinicians and specialty-trained emergency and trauma nurses. [Level V]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: With longer durations of MMF, some physical therapy may be required to restore range of motion after treatment is complete, including use of a Therabite device or similar. In the event of mild postoperative malocclusion, orthodontic care may help to return the patient to premorbid occlusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Mandibular Angle Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular angle reduction surgery is a technically challenging procedure due to access constraints and the need for symmetric osteotomies between the left and right sides. Approaching the mandibular angles via an extraoral approach will place a scar on the neck and risks facial nerve injury. In contrast, the intraoral approach severely limits visualization of the area in question and makes achieving a symmetric result difficult. When an extraoral approach is selected, it often accompanies a face or neck lift because the area is already exposed via a Blair incision. From this approach, the superficial musculoaponeurotic system must be incised to expose the masseter muscle, which envelops the angle of the mandible. Care is necessary during this portion of the procedure because the facial nerve's marginal mandibular and cervical branches often overlie the mandible in this area. In the region of the mandibular angle, posterior to the gonial notch, the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve lies superior to the inferior border of the mandible in 80% of patients and inferior to it in 20%; anterior to the gonial notch, where the facial artery crosses the mandible, the marginal mandibular branch is consistently superior to the inferior border of the mandible. [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Mandibular Angle Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical branch of the facial nerve lies inferior to the marginal mandibular branch. It is consistently found 1 cm below the halfway point of a line drawn between the mentum and the mastoid tip, although proximally in its course, it may share a common root with the marginal mandibular branch. [38] A nerve stimulator will help identify motor nerve branches and may prevent iatrogenic injuries, provided the patient has not received paralytic drugs. Once the masseter has been exposed safely, it can be incised along the posterior border of the mandibular angle and elevated off the bone. A reciprocating saw is then used to perform the osteotomy. The medial pterygoid muscle is elevated from the medial surface of the bone fragment to liberate it completely.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Before arrival in the hospital, there are no established universal protocols for prehospital care of mandibular dislocation. The basis for a transport decision is on patient stability and other factors that may have contributed to the injury, for example, trauma, shock, or altered mental status. So long as the patient has no airway symptoms, transport can proceed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones), the Arabic phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower limbs), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of fracture of the upper limbs), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق فخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all associated with “كسر,” the Arabic term for fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 799
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Fractuur van schedel of aangezichtsbeenderen, deel niet gespecificeerd” is classified under the parent entity “كسر في قبو القحف” and is identified as involving the “obličejové kosti”. This fracture corresponds to the broader category of “skull and face fractures”, which map to the Arabic term “كسر في قبو القحف”. Clinical management of this injury is directed at the “obličejové kosti” as the direct procedure site for “treatment; facial fracture or dislocation”.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2396
retrieved_docs.content: The late effects of a fracture are closely linked to the original injury across multiple anatomical sites. The “Late effect of fracture of spine AND/OR trunk without spinal cord lesion” is associated with a fracture (كسر), indicating that patients may develop delayed complications even when the spinal cord is spared. Similarly, the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of skull and facial bone fractures) are associated with a fracture (كسر), underscoring the potential for prolonged sequelae in craniofacial injuries. In Arabic, the phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of lower‑extremity fractures), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of upper‑extremity fractures), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ” (late effects of femoral‑neck fractures) are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). Collectively, these relationships emphasize that delayed or late‑onset manifestations are a common aspect of fracture healing and management, regardless of whether the injury involves the spine, trunk, skull, facial bones, upper or lower limbs, or the femoral neck. Recognizing these late effects is essential for comprehensive patient care and for monitoring potential complications after the acute phase of a fracture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.
